title: AutoProxy: AI Message Intelligence Architecture
description: Buildable specification for AutoProxy's AI/ML layer covering model selection, prompt design, RAG with pgvector, classification pipeline, cost modeling, and Elixir process topology.
tags: autoproxy, ai, llm, architecture, elixir, pgvector, rag, classification, cost-modeling
excerpt:
AutoProxy: AI Message Intelligence Architecture

Executive Summary

AutoProxy is a message intelligence layer that intercepts every message in the Amos Comms domain, classifies it, routes it, and optionally generates or suggests responses. It operates in two modes: on-site (single property operations) and corporate/HQ (multi-property oversight). The system is built entirely in Elixir, uses external LLMs via HTTP APIs, and stores all knowledge in Postgres (including pgvector for embeddings).

Executive Summary

AutoProxy targets a real, well-documented pain point with strong tailwinds. The competitive landscape is evolving fast, but the hospitality-vertical angle is the primary strategic advantage. Generic AI assistants increase noise and cognitive load; a domain-aware proxy that understands hospitality operations can actually reduce it. That distinction is the entire thesis.

1. Problem Statement

The Shoulder-Tapping Tax

Hospitality operations run on interruptions. Every question, status update, and request requires finding the right person and pulling them out of whatever they are doing. This tax compounds at every level of the organization:

**On-Site (Property Level)**
- A housekeeper needs to know if a late checkout was approved. She walks to the front desk. The front desk agent

AutoProxy — Product Research

**Concept:** An AI-powered message proxy layer for both on-site hotel operations and corporate/HQ teams. Routes messages intelligently, filters noise, suggests responses, auto-answers high-confidence factual queries, detects cross-property patterns, triages reports, and generates executive digests. Your personal secretary — whether you're on the floor or in the boardroom.

**Positioning:** "You only see the messages that need you."

AutoProxy Technical Specification

Status: Draft

Problem Statement

Hospitality operations suffer from constant "shoulder tapping" -- staff interrupt each other with questions that could be answered from existing knowledge, routed to the right person automatically, or batched until a natural break. The result is fragmented attention, delayed responses, and operational overhead that scales linearly with headcount.

AutoProxy is an intelligent message proxy layer that sits inside Amos's Comms dom

This revision extends the architecture to serve **corporate/HQ use cases** where regional managers and executives oversee multiple properties through a single pane of glass.

title: Amos (Stacker): Project README
description: Overview of the Amos hospitality PMS built with Ash Framework on Elixir/Phoenix, including setup instructions, domain architecture, and project structure.
tags: amos, stacker, pms, hospitality, elixir, phoenix, ash-framework, readme
excerpt:
Amos

> Hospitality property management system built with the Ash Framework on Elixir/Phoenix.

Amos is a ground-up rebuild of a prior NestJS system ("Stacker"), preserving the domain model while leveraging Ash's declarative resource system. It serves as the single source of truth for niche hospitality operations -- a single-business PMS, not a multi-tenant SaaS platform.

This repository contains the API backend only. No frontend code lives here.

Tech Stack

- **Elixir 1.18+** / **Phoenix 1.8*

Comms Domain — Deferred Work

Phase 13: Real-time & Notifications

Phase 12 builds the data model only. Phase 13 adds the real-time layer:

- **Ash.Notifier.PubSub** on Message (publish create/destroy to channel topics)
- **Phoenix Channels** — `UserSocket` (JWT auth), `ChannelChannel` (per-channel topic handler)
- **Phoenix.Presence** — online/offline tracking, typing indicators

1. Phoenix Channels vs LiveView for Real-Time

Recommendation: Phoenix Channels (not LiveView)
